name: USCRI Humanitarian Legal Services
organization: US Committee for Refugees and Immigrants
description: USCRI's legal department provides low- to no-cost professional legal representation to low-income refugees and immigrants. Services cover family- and humanitarian-based immigration matters before USCIS, Immigration Courts, and the Board of Immigration Appeals. The program assists individuals navigating complex asylum, resettlement, and family reunification processes.
eligibility: Low-income refugees and immigrants with family- or humanitarian-based immigration matters.
applicationProcess: Contact USCRI directly through their website to request legal services.
documentsRequired: Immigration documents, proof of refugee or immigrant status, income verification

name: National Immigrant Justice Center — Free Legal Defense
description: The National Immigrant Justice Center (NIJC) defends the rights of immigrants, refugees, and asylum seekers through free legal representation. NIJC focuses on keeping families together, freeing people from immigration detention, and preventing wrongful deportations. They serve thousands of clients annually across the US through direct legal services and advocacy.
eligibility: Immigrants, refugees, asylum seekers, and individuals in immigration detention in the US.
applicationProcess: Apply for legal assistance through the NIJC website or contact their intake line.
documentsRequired: Immigration documents, case history, proof of identity

name: The Advocates for Human Rights — Free Legal Help
description: The Advocates for Human Rights provides free legal assistance to people seeking asylum, unaccompanied children, people in immigration detention, and survivors of human trafficking. Their volunteer attorneys and staff represent clients in immigration court proceedings and USCIS interviews. The organization operates pro bono legal clinics and coordinates with law firm partners to expand access to justice.
eligibility: Asylum seekers, unaccompanied minors, immigration detainees, and trafficking survivors.
applicationProcess: Submit a request for legal help through their website. Priority given to asylum seekers and detained individuals.
geographicScope: United States - primarily Minnesota, with national reach
documentsRequired: Immigration case documents, proof of identity, court notices if applicable

name: New York State Legal Services for Newly Arrived Immigrants
organization: New York State Office for New Americans
description: Governor Hochul's $3 million legal services grant program provides newly arrived immigrants in New York City with free immigration-related legal services. The program is administered over three years and supports organizations offering representation in immigration proceedings, DACA renewals, and status adjustments. It is designed to help migrants navigate the legal system and avoid deportation.
eligibility: Newly arrived immigrants and migrants residing in New York City.
applicationProcess: Services are provided through funded organizations. Contact the NY Office for New Americans to find a participating provider.
documentsRequired: Immigration documents, proof of NYC residency

name: Washington State Legal Aid for Immigrants
description: The Washington State Department of Commerce funds legal aid services specifically for immigrants across Washington State. The program supports nonprofit legal organizations that provide free civil legal assistance to low-income immigrants, including help with immigration status, family-based petitions, and protection from exploitation. Funding is distributed through competitive grants to service providers.
eligibility: Low-income immigrants residing in Washington State seeking civil legal assistance.
applicationProcess: Find a funded legal aid provider through the Washington State Department of Commerce website.
documentsRequired: Immigration documents, proof of Washington State residency, income verification

name: Canada Resettlement Assistance Program (RAP)
description: Canada's Resettlement Assistance Program (RAP) provides government-funded support to resettled refugees during their first year in Canada. The program covers immediate essential needs including temporary housing, basic household items, orientation to Canadian society, and referrals to other services. RAP is delivered through a network of service provider organizations across Canadian cities.
eligibility: Government-Assisted Refugees (GARs) and Blended Visa Office-Referred Refugees (BVORs) in their first year of resettlement in Canada.
amount: Varies by family size and location
applicationProcess: RAP services are provided automatically to eligible resettled refugees upon arrival through designated service provider organizations.
documentsRequired: Refugee travel document, proof of refugee status, Canadian immigration paperwork

name: ORR Refugee Benefits — Food, Medical & Cash Assistance
organization: Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR), US Department of HHS
description: The US Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR) provides comprehensive benefits to newly arrived refugees including food assistance through SNAP, healthcare through Medicaid, and cash assistance through Refugee Cash Assistance (RCA) or Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF). ORR funds state governments, resettlement agencies, and nonprofits to deliver these services to eligible individuals during their initial resettlement period.
eligibility: Refugees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, Amerasians, victims of trafficking, and Special Immigrant Visa holders within their eligibility window (typically 8 months to 5 years depending on benefit type).
amount: Varies by benefit type and family size
applicationProcess: Apply through your state refugee agency or resettlement organization upon arrival in the US. ORR-funded providers will assist with enrollment.
documentsRequired: Refugee travel document, I-94 arrival record, proof of refugee or asylee status, Social Security Number (if available)

name: JCFS HIAS Legal Aid & Advocacy for Refugees
description: JCFS Chicago, a HIAS affiliate, provides legal aid and advocacy services to refugees and immigrants, including assistance with asylum applications, family reunification petitions, and immigration court preparation. The organization connects clients with pro bono attorneys and accredited representatives. Services are provided on a sliding scale or free of charge based on financial need.
eligibility: Refugees and immigrants in the Chicago area seeking immigration legal assistance.
applicationProcess: Contact JCFS directly to schedule an intake appointment. Services are prioritized based on urgency and legal need.
geographicScope: Chicago metropolitan area, Illinois, United States
documentsRequired: Immigration documents, proof of refugee or immigrant status, court notices if applicable
